title: Today's Tech Brief: Used Waymo robotaxi batteries become backup storage for power grids
summary: A look at used Waymo robotaxi batteries becoming backup storage and Jeff Bezos funding a brain algorithm search.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Used Waymo robotaxi batteries become backup storage for power grids
imageCaption: Used Waymo robotaxi batteries become backup storage for power grids
imageSourceTitle: Used Waymo robotaxi batteries become backup storage for power grids

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. First up, Waymo's robotaxi fleet is getting a second life for its batteries. Thousands of electric vehicles in the autonomous fleet may eventually see their batteries repurposed as backup storage for power grids. This is a clever move, as these batteries still have plenty of life left in them after powering the robotaxis. It's like giving old car batteries a retirement plan.

The idea is to use these batteries to help stabilize the grid during periods of high demand or when there's a power outage. This approach could also help reduce waste and make the most of the resources we've already invested in these batteries. I am looking at the potential for this to become a bigger trend in the future, as more and more companies look for creative ways to reuse and recycle materials.

Also in the news, Jeff Bezos is funding a project to find the brain's 'core algorithm'. This sounds like a wild goose chase, but the idea is to understand the fundamental processes that govern how our brains work. It's an ambitious goal, and I'm curious to see where this research takes us. The brain is still one of the most mysterious and complex systems we know of, so any progress here could be fascinating.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
